+## Question 2: LoadProgram and Yalnix Demand Paging
+
+### Overview
+
+To implement demand paging in the Yalnix kernel, we modify `LoadProgram` so that it does **not** read any text or data pages into physical memory. Instead, it records where each page's data resides on disk and marks all page table entries as invalid. When the process later accesses any of these pages, a page fault (TRAP_MEMORY) occurs, and the kernel fault handler reads just that single page from disk into a newly allocated physical frame.
+
+### Modified / New Data Structures
+
+```c
+/*
+ * NEW: Per-page backing store information.
+ * Tracks where to read a page's contents from on a demand-page fault.
+ */
+struct page_backing_info {
+    int is_file_backed;    /* 1 = backed by program file on disk, 0 = zero-fill */
+    int file_id;           /* Identifier (inode number) of the program file */
+    int file_offset;       /* Byte offset within the program file for this page */
+    int prot;              /* Protection bits (PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE|PROT_EXEC) */
+    int is_mapped;         /* 1 = this vpn is part of the process's address space */
+};
+
+/*
+ * MODIFIED: Process Control Block (PCB).
+ * Added backing[] array for demand paging metadata.
+ */
+struct pcb {
+    int pid;
+    SavedContext ctx;
+    struct pte *page_table;          /* Region 0 page table (array of PTEs) */
+    int text_start_vpn;              /* First virtual page of text segment */
+    int text_end_vpn;                /* Last virtual page of text segment */
+    int data_start_vpn;              /* First virtual page of data segment */
+    int brk_vpn;                     /* Current break (top of heap) virtual page */
+    int stack_base_vpn;              /* Base (lowest address) of user stack */
+
+    /* NEW: backing info for each virtual page in Region 0 */
+    struct page_backing_info backing[MAX_PT_LEN];
+};
+```
+
+In the Yalnix RCS 421 hardware, each page table entry (PTE) has the existing structure:
+```c
+struct pte {
+    u_long valid : 1;   /* 1 = page is in physical memory */
+    u_long prot  : 3;   /* protection bits */
+    u_long pfn   : 24;  /* physical frame number */
+};
+```
+
+We also reserve a kernel virtual page for temporary mappings during fault handling:
+
+```c
+/* A single reserved virtual page in Region 1 (kernel space) for temporary I/O mapping */
+#define KERNEL_TEMP_VPN  (VMEM_1_LIMIT >> PAGESHIFT) - 1
+    /* This is a VIRTUAL page number in the kernel's Region 1 address space */
+```
+
+### Modified LoadProgram (Pseudo-code)
+
+Instead of allocating frames and reading text/data from the program file, `LoadProgram` now sets up the page table entries as **invalid** and records backing store metadata:
+
+```
+LoadProgram(pcb, filename):
+    /* Parse the executable header to determine segment layout (same as before) */
+    header = read_exec_header(filename)
+    text_start_vaddr = header.text_vaddr         /* VIRTUAL ADDRESS */
+    text_size = header.text_size
+    data_start_vaddr = header.data_vaddr         /* VIRTUAL ADDRESS */
+    data_size = header.data_size
+    bss_start_vaddr = data_start_vaddr + data_size  /* VIRTUAL ADDRESS */
+    bss_size = header.bss_size
+
+    /* Convert virtual addresses to virtual page numbers */
+    text_start_vpn = text_start_vaddr >> PAGESHIFT     /* VIRTUAL page number */
+    text_end_vpn = (text_start_vaddr + text_size - 1) >> PAGESHIFT  /* VIRTUAL page number */
+    data_start_vpn = data_start_vaddr >> PAGESHIFT     /* VIRTUAL page number */
+    data_end_vpn = (data_start_vaddr + data_size - 1) >> PAGESHIFT  /* VIRTUAL page number */
+    bss_start_vpn = bss_start_vaddr >> PAGESHIFT       /* VIRTUAL page number */
+    bss_end_vpn = (bss_start_vaddr + bss_size - 1) >> PAGESHIFT    /* VIRTUAL page number */
+
+    /* Set up stack region */
+    stack_base_vpn = USER_STACK_LIMIT >> PAGESHIFT     /* VIRTUAL page number */
+    /* (Stack grows down; initial sp set from header) */
+
+    /* Free any previously allocated frames for this process (if exec) */
+    for vpn = 0 to MAX_PT_LEN - 1:          /* vpn is a VIRTUAL page number */
+        if pcb->page_table[vpn].valid == 1:
+            free_frame(pcb->page_table[vpn].pfn)    /* pfn is PHYSICAL frame number */
+            pcb->page_table[vpn].valid = 0
+
+    /* Initialize all backing info to unmapped */
+    for vpn = 0 to MAX_PT_LEN - 1:
+        pcb->backing[vpn].is_mapped = 0
+
+    /* ---- TEXT SEGMENT: file-backed, read+exec ---- */
+    file_offset = header.text_file_offset
+    for vpn = text_start_vpn to text_end_vpn:    /* VIRTUAL page numbers */
+        pcb->page_table[vpn].valid = 0           /* NOT in physical memory yet */
+        pcb->page_table[vpn].prot = PROT_NONE
+        pcb->backing[vpn].is_file_backed = 1
+        pcb->backing[vpn].file_id = inode_of(filename)
+        pcb->backing[vpn].file_offset = file_offset
+        pcb->backing[vpn].prot = (PROT_READ | PROT_EXEC)
+        pcb->backing[vpn].is_mapped = 1
+        file_offset += PAGESIZE
+
+    /* ---- INITIALIZED DATA SEGMENT: file-backed, read+write ---- */
+    file_offset = header.data_file_offset
+    for vpn = data_start_vpn to data_end_vpn:    /* VIRTUAL page numbers */
+        pcb->page_table[vpn].valid = 0
+        pcb->page_table[vpn].prot = PROT_NONE
+        pcb->backing[vpn].is_file_backed = 1
+        pcb->backing[vpn].file_id = inode_of(filename)
+        pcb->backing[vpn].file_offset = file_offset
+        pcb->backing[vpn].prot = (PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE)
+        pcb->backing[vpn].is_mapped = 1
+        file_offset += PAGESIZE
+
+    /* ---- BSS SEGMENT: zero-fill on demand, read+write ---- */
+    for vpn = bss_start_vpn to bss_end_vpn:      /* VIRTUAL page numbers */
+        pcb->page_table[vpn].valid = 0
+        pcb->page_table[vpn].prot = PROT_NONE
+        pcb->backing[vpn].is_file_backed = 0     /* Zero-fill, not file-backed */
+        pcb->backing[vpn].prot = (PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE)
+        pcb->backing[vpn].is_mapped = 1
+
+    /* ---- USER STACK: zero-fill on demand, read+write ---- */
+    for vpn = stack_base_vpn to (VMEM_1_BASE >> PAGESHIFT) - 1:  /* VIRTUAL page numbers */
+        pcb->page_table[vpn].valid = 0
+        pcb->page_table[vpn].prot = PROT_NONE
+        pcb->backing[vpn].is_file_backed = 0
+        pcb->backing[vpn].prot = (PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE)
+        pcb->backing[vpn].is_mapped = 1
+
+    /* Record segment boundaries in PCB */
+    pcb->text_start_vpn = text_start_vpn
+    pcb->text_end_vpn = text_end_vpn
+    pcb->data_start_vpn = data_start_vpn
+    pcb->brk_vpn = bss_end_vpn + 1
+    pcb->stack_base_vpn = stack_base_vpn
+
+    /* Set initial PC and SP from the executable header (same as before) */
+    pcb->ctx.pc = header.entry_point       /* VIRTUAL ADDRESS */
+    pcb->ctx.sp = USER_STACK_LIMIT         /* VIRTUAL ADDRESS */
+
+    /* Flush the TLB for Region 0 since all PTEs changed */
+    WriteRegister(REG_TLB_FLUSH, TLB_FLUSH_0)
+
+    /* DO NOT read any pages from disk — they will be demand-paged */
+    return SUCCESS
+```
+
+### Page Fault Handler (TRAP_MEMORY)
+
+When the process tries to access any of these invalid pages, the hardware generates a TRAP_MEMORY. The kernel distinguishes a demand-page fault (page is in a valid region but not yet loaded) from an illegal access:
+
+```
+trap_memory_handler(ExceptionInfo *info):
+    faulting_vaddr = info->addr              /* VIRTUAL ADDRESS that caused the fault */
+    vpn = faulting_vaddr >> PAGESHIFT        /* VIRTUAL page number */
+
+    pcb = current_process
+
+    /* ---- Step 1: Validate the access ---- */
+
+    /* Check if this virtual page is within the process's mapped address space */
+    if vpn < 0 or vpn >= MAX_PT_LEN:
+        /* Address completely out of range — kill process */
+        exit_process(pcb, ERROR)
+        return
+
+    if pcb->backing[vpn].is_mapped == 0:
+        /* This virtual page is not part of any segment — illegal access */
+        exit_process(pcb, ERROR)
+        return
+
+    if pcb->page_table[vpn].valid == 1:
+        /* Page is already valid — this must be a protection violation */
+        /* (e.g., writing to read-only text page) */
+        exit_process(pcb, ERROR)
+        return
+
+    /* ---- Step 2: This is a legitimate demand-page fault ---- */
+
+    /* Allocate a free physical frame */
+    pfn = allocate_free_frame()              /* PHYSICAL frame number */
+    if pfn == -1:
+        /* Out of physical memory — would need page replacement */
+        /* (not required for this question; could kill process) */
+        exit_process(pcb, ERROR)
+        return
+
+    /* ---- Step 3: Fill the physical frame with correct content ---- */
+
+    /*
+     * We need to write to the physical frame identified by pfn.
+     * But the kernel operates using virtual addresses, so we must
+     * temporarily map this physical frame into a kernel virtual page
+     * to be able to read/write its contents.
+     */
+    temp_vpn = KERNEL_TEMP_VPN               /* VIRTUAL page number in Region 1 */
+    temp_idx = temp_vpn - (VMEM_1_BASE >> PAGESHIFT)  /* Index into Region 1 page table */
+
+    /* Create temporary mapping: kernel virtual page -> new physical frame */
+    kernel_page_table[temp_idx].valid = 1
+    kernel_page_table[temp_idx].prot = (PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE)
+    kernel_page_table[temp_idx].pfn = pfn    /* PHYSICAL frame number */
+    WriteRegister(REG_TLB_FLUSH, temp_vpn << PAGESHIFT)  /* Flush VIRTUAL ADDRESS */
+
+    temp_vaddr = temp_vpn << PAGESHIFT       /* VIRTUAL ADDRESS in kernel space */
+
+    if pcb->backing[vpn].is_file_backed == 1:
+        /*
+         * Read this page's contents from the program file on disk.
+         * We read from the file at the recorded offset into the
+         * kernel virtual address that maps to the new physical frame.
+         */
+        ReadFromDisk(
+            pcb->backing[vpn].file_id,       /* which file (inode) */
+            pcb->backing[vpn].file_offset,   /* byte offset in file */
+            (void *) temp_vaddr,             /* destination: VIRTUAL ADDRESS in kernel */
+            PAGESIZE                         /* read one full page */
+        )
+    else:
+        /*
+         * BSS or stack page — zero-fill the frame.
+         * Write zeros to the kernel virtual address that maps to the physical frame.
+         */
+        memset((void *) temp_vaddr, 0, PAGESIZE)  /* VIRTUAL ADDRESS in kernel */
+
+    /* Remove the temporary kernel mapping */
+    kernel_page_table[temp_idx].valid = 0
+    WriteRegister(REG_TLB_FLUSH, temp_vpn << PAGESHIFT)  /* Flush VIRTUAL ADDRESS */
+
+    /* ---- Step 4: Update the user's page table entry ---- */
+
+    pcb->page_table[vpn].valid = 1
+    pcb->page_table[vpn].pfn = pfn          /* PHYSICAL frame number */
+    pcb->page_table[vpn].prot = pcb->backing[vpn].prot
+
+    /* Flush the TLB entry for the user's faulting virtual page */
+    WriteRegister(REG_TLB_FLUSH, faulting_vaddr & ~(PAGESIZE - 1))  /* VIRTUAL ADDRESS */
+
+    /* ---- Return to user process ---- */
+    /* The hardware will re-execute the faulting instruction, which now succeeds */
+    return
+```
+
+### Summary of Address Types
+
+| Variable | Type | Description |
+|----------|------|-------------|
+| `faulting_vaddr` | **Virtual address** | The user-space address that caused the fault |
+| `vpn` | **Virtual page number** | User-space virtual page derived from faulting_vaddr |
+| `pfn` | **Physical frame number** | Newly allocated physical frame from the free list |
+| `temp_vpn` | **Virtual page number** | Kernel Region 1 virtual page used for temporary I/O mapping |
+| `temp_vaddr` | **Virtual address** | Kernel virtual address = temp_vpn << PAGESHIFT |
+| `temp_idx` | Index | Index into the kernel's Region 1 page table array |
+| `file_offset` | Byte offset | Position within the program file on disk (not an address) |
+| `text_start_vpn`, `data_start_vpn`, etc. | **Virtual page numbers** | Boundaries of user address space segments |
+
+### Key Design Points
+
+1. **Initialization causes faults**: By setting `valid = 0` for all text, data, BSS, and stack pages in `LoadProgram`, any access to these pages will cause the hardware to generate a TRAP_MEMORY before the process can read or execute any instruction from them — including the very first instruction at the entry point.
+
+2. **Recognizing demand-page faults**: The handler checks `backing[vpn].is_mapped` to distinguish a demand-page fault (mapped but not yet loaded) from a true illegal access (unmapped region such as the gap between heap and stack).
+
+3. **Temporary kernel mapping for I/O**: Since the kernel uses virtual addresses, we cannot directly write to a physical frame by physical address. We temporarily map the new physical frame into a known kernel virtual page, perform the read or zero-fill via that kernel virtual address, then unmap it.
+
+4. **Pages never accessed are never loaded**: A text or data page that the process never touches during its execution will never cause a fault and will therefore never consume a physical frame.
+

+## Question 3: Page Replacement Algorithms

+**Physical memory:** 4 frames, initially empty.

+### 3.1 Optimal Replacement
+
+Optimal replaces the page whose next use is **farthest in the future** (or never used again).
+
+| Step | Ref | Memory Contents | Fault? | Evicted | Reason |
+|------|-----|-----------------|--------|---------|--------|
+| 1 | **3** | {3, -, -, -} | **FAULT** | — | Cold start; frame available |
+| 2 | **5** | {3, 5, -, -} | **FAULT** | — | Cold start; frame available |
+| 3 | **0** | {3, 5, 0, -} | **FAULT** | — | Cold start; frame available |
+| 4 | **1** | {3, 5, 0, 1} | **FAULT** | — | Cold start; frame available |
+| 5 | 0 | {3, 5, 0, 1} | Hit | — | 0 already in memory |
+| 6 | **2** | {3, 2, 0, 1} | **FAULT** | **5** | Future: 3→step9, **5→step14(farthest)**, 0→step8, 1→step7. Evict 5. |
+| 7 | 1 | {3, 2, 0, 1} | Hit | — | 1 already in memory |
+| 8 | 0 | {3, 2, 0, 1} | Hit | — | 0 already in memory |
+| 9 | 3 | {3, 2, 0, 1} | Hit | — | 3 already in memory |
+| 10 | 2 | {3, 2, 0, 1} | Hit | — | 2 already in memory |
+| 11 | **4** | {4, 2, 0, 1} | **FAULT** | **3** | Future: **3→never(∞)**, 2→step12, 0→step13, **1→never(∞)**. Evict 3 (or 1; both never used again). |
+| 12 | 2 | {4, 2, 0, 1} | Hit | — | 2 already in memory |
+| 13 | 0 | {4, 2, 0, 1} | Hit | — | 0 already in memory |
+| 14 | **5** | {4, 2, 0, 5} | **FAULT** | **1** | Future: all pages never used again. Evict 1 (arbitrary). |

+**Total page faults: 7**

+Faults occurred on references to virtual pages: **3, 5, 0, 1, 2, 4, 5** (at steps 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 11, 14).

+
+FIFO replaces the page that has been in memory the **longest** (first in, first out).

+## Question 4: Unix File System Extension
+
+### Problem Recap
+
+We must support a second, independent "alternate" collection of data bytes for any file, without modifying the on-disk format of directory entries (16-bit inode number + 14-char filename) or inodes. The solution must be space-efficient: files that don't use an alternate collection should incur no overhead.
+
+### Design
+
+#### 1. The Alternate-Data Map Directory
+
+We designate a **special, reserved inode** — say **inode number 1** (which in classical Unix is traditionally reserved and unused for regular files) — to serve as the **alternate-data map directory**. This directory is an internal file system data structure; it does not appear in any user-visible directory listing and cannot be accessed by pathname.
+
+This directory uses the **standard directory entry format** (16-bit inode number + 14-char name field) — we do not modify the format. Each entry in this directory maps an original file's inode number to an alternate inode:
+
+- **Name field (14 chars):** Stores the decimal ASCII representation of the original file's inode number (e.g., `"317"` for inode 317). Since inode numbers are 16-bit (max 65535), this requires at most 5 characters, well within the 14-character limit.
+- **Inode number field (16 bits):** Points to the **alternate inode** — a separate, independently allocated inode that manages the alternate data collection for that file.
+
+For example, if file with inode 317 has an alternate data collection stored in inode 4021, then the alt-map directory contains an entry:
+
+```
+| inode: 4021 | name: "317\0..."  |
+```
+
+#### 2. Alternate Inodes
+
+When a file first needs an alternate data collection, the file system allocates a **new inode** from the standard free inode pool. This alternate inode is a perfectly normal inode — it has its own size field, direct block pointers, single/double/triple indirect block pointers, and so forth — it just stores the alternate data instead of a regular file's data. The inode format is completely unchanged.
+
+The alternate inode's data blocks are allocated from the standard free data block pool, entirely independent of the main inode's data blocks. Each collection has its own independent size.
+
+#### 3. How It Works
+
+**Creating or opening the alternate data collection for a file with inode N:**
+
+1. Look up the alt-map directory (inode 1) for an entry whose name field encodes inode number N.
+2. **If found:** The inode number in that directory entry is the alternate inode M. Open inode M and return a file descriptor for it. All subsequent read/write operations proceed exactly as for any normal file, using inode M's block pointers and size.
+3. **If not found (first access):** Allocate a new inode M from the free inode list. Initialize M with size = 0 and no allocated blocks. Add a new entry to the alt-map directory: name = ASCII representation of N, inode = M. Then open inode M and return a file descriptor.
+
+**Opening the main data collection:**
+
+This works exactly as in the unmodified classical Unix file system — the directory entry points directly to the file's main inode, and I/O uses that inode's block pointers. No change needed.
+
+**Reading/writing either collection:**
+
+Once a file descriptor is associated with either the main inode or the alternate inode, all I/O operations (read, write, seek, truncate) work identically through the standard inode block-pointer mechanism. The two collections are entirely independent because they use separate inodes with separate block pointers and separate sizes.
+
+#### 4. File Deletion
+
+When a file's main inode is freed (i.e., its link count drops to 0 and no processes have it open):
+
+1. Check the alt-map directory for an entry whose name matches this inode number.
+2. If an entry exists, free the alternate inode and all its data blocks, then remove the entry from the alt-map directory.
+3. Free the main inode and its data blocks as usual.
+
+This ensures no orphaned alternate inodes or data blocks are left behind.
+
+#### 5. Space Efficiency
+
+- Files without an alternate data collection have **zero overhead**: no extra inode is allocated, and there is no entry in the alt-map directory.
+- Only files that actually use the alternate collection get an entry in the alt-map directory (one 16-byte directory entry) and one additional inode.
+- The alt-map directory itself grows dynamically using the standard directory expansion mechanism (allocating additional data blocks as needed), so it uses minimal space when few files have alternate data.
+
+#### 6. Hard Links
+
+Since the alternate data is associated with the **inode** (not with any particular directory entry or pathname), multiple hard links to the same file naturally share the same alternate data collection. This is correct behavior — the alternate data is a property of the file, not of a name for the file.
+
+#### 7. Summary of On-Disk Changes
+
+| Component | Modified? | Notes |
+|-----------|-----------|-------|
+| Directory entry format | **No** | 16-bit inode + 14-char name unchanged |
+| Inode format | **No** | Standard inode format unchanged |
+| Superblock | **Minor** | Record inode 1 as alt-map directory (or simply convention) |
+| Alt-map directory (inode 1) | **New usage** | Standard directory using existing format; maps original inode → alternate inode |
+| Alternate inodes | **New allocation** | Standard inodes allocated from free list on demand |
+| Data blocks for alt data | **New allocation** | Standard data blocks allocated from free list on demand |
+

+## Question 5: Cryptographic Message Protection
+
+### Goal
+
+Design a scheme so that the receiver of a message can verify:
+1. **Authenticity**: The message was sent by the person it claims to be from.
+2. **Integrity**: The message contents have not been modified in transit.
+
+### Cryptographic Primitives Used
+
+- **Asymmetric (public-key) cryptography** (e.g., RSA): Each user has a key pair (public key, private key). Data signed with a private key can only be verified with the corresponding public key.
+- **Cryptographic hash function** (e.g., SHA-256): Produces a fixed-size message digest from an arbitrary-length input. Computationally infeasible to find collisions or reverse.
+- **Digital certificates**: A trusted Certificate Authority (CA) signs each user's public key, binding it to their identity.
+
+### Setup / Initialization
+
+Each user process is initialized with the following small, fixed amount of state:
+
+1. **Its own user's private key** `SK_self` (for signing outgoing messages)
+2. **Its own user's certificate** `Cert_self` = { identity_self, PK_self, Sig_CA(identity_self || PK_self) } — the user's public key signed by the CA, binding the public key to the user's identity
+3. **The CA's public key** `PK_CA` (for verifying other users' certificates)
+
+No per-destination keys are needed at initialization. The sender's certificate is transmitted with the message so the receiver can obtain the sender's public key.
+
+### Sending a Message (Pseudo-code)
+
+```
+SEND(message M, destination):
+    /* Sender's identity and keys are pre-initialized */
+    S = identity_self
+    SK_S = private_key_self
+    Cert_S = certificate_self    /* = (S, PK_S, Sig_CA(S || PK_S)) */
+
+    /* Step 1: Compute the message digest (hash) for efficiency.
+     * This avoids applying the expensive asymmetric signature
+     * operation to the entire (potentially very large) message.
+     */
+    h = HASH(M)                  /* Fixed-size digest, e.g., 256 bits */
+
+    /* Step 2: Sign the digest with the sender's private key.
+     * Only the holder of SK_S can produce this signature.
+     */
+    sig = SIGN(h, SK_S)          /* Asymmetric signature on fixed-size digest: fast */
+
+    /* Step 3: Construct and send the transmitted message.
+     * Include the sender's identity, the original message, the signature,
+     * and the sender's certificate (so the receiver can obtain PK_S).
+     */
+    transmitted_message = (S, M, sig, Cert_S)
+    NETWORK_SEND(transmitted_message, destination)
+```
+
+### Receiving and Verifying a Message (Pseudo-code)
+
+```
+RECEIVE():
+    /* Receive the message from the network */
+    (claimed_sender, M, sig, cert) = NETWORK_RECEIVE()
+    /* cert = (cert_identity, cert_PK, cert_CA_sig) */
+
+    /* ---- Step 1: Verify the sender's certificate ---- */
+    /* Check that the CA actually signed this (identity, public key) binding.
+     * This ensures cert_PK genuinely belongs to cert_identity.
+     */
+    cert_digest = HASH(cert.identity || cert.PK)
+    if not VERIFY(cert_digest, cert.CA_sig, PK_CA):
+        REJECT("Certificate is forged or corrupted")
+        return
+
+    /* ---- Step 2: Verify sender identity consistency ---- */
+    /* The identity in the certificate must match the claimed sender */
+    if cert.identity != claimed_sender:
+        REJECT("Claimed sender does not match certificate identity")
+        return
+
+    /* At this point, we have a verified binding:
+     * cert.PK is authentically the public key of claimed_sender */
+    PK_sender = cert.PK
+
+    /* ---- Step 3: Verify message integrity and authenticity ---- */
+    /* Recompute the hash of the received message */
+    h = HASH(M)
+
+    /* Verify the signature using the sender's authenticated public key */
+    if not VERIFY(h, sig, PK_sender):
+        REJECT("Signature invalid: message was tampered with, or not sent by claimed sender")
+        return
+
+    /* ---- Both properties verified ---- */
+    /*
+     * AUTHENTICITY: Only the holder of the private key corresponding to
+     * PK_sender could have produced sig. The certificate (verified by the CA)
+     * confirms PK_sender belongs to claimed_sender. Therefore, the message
+     * was signed by claimed_sender.
+     *
+     * INTEGRITY: If any bit of M were modified in transit, HASH(M) would
+     * differ from the hash that was originally signed, causing VERIFY to fail.
+     * Therefore, M has not been modified since it was signed.
+     */
+    ACCEPT(M, claimed_sender)
+```
+
+### Computational Efficiency Analysis
+
+The scheme is designed for efficiency, especially for large messages:
+
+- **Hashing** (`HASH(M)`): O(|M|) — linear in message size, but the hash function is very fast (processes data at memory-bandwidth speeds).
+- **Signing** (`SIGN(h, SK)`): O(1) — operates on the fixed-size hash digest (e.g., 256 bits), not on the full message. A single asymmetric operation.
+- **Verifying** (`VERIFY(h, sig, PK)`): O(1) — also operates on the fixed-size digest. A single asymmetric operation.
+- **Certificate verification**: O(1) — one hash and one verify of a small, fixed-size certificate.
+
+If we instead signed the entire message M directly (without hashing first), the signature operation would need to process all of M using expensive modular exponentiation, making it orders of magnitude slower for large messages. The hash-then-sign approach reduces the expensive asymmetric operation to a single fixed-cost step regardless of message size.
+
+### Optimization for Repeated Communication
+
+Since the problem notes that a sender may send many messages to the same destination, the receiver can **cache verified public keys**:
+
+```
+/* Maintained by the receiving process across messages */
+verified_keys = {}    /* Map: sender identity -> verified public key */
+
+RECEIVE_OPTIMIZED():
+    (claimed_sender, M, sig, cert_or_null) = NETWORK_RECEIVE()
+
+    if claimed_sender in verified_keys:
+        /* We already verified this sender's certificate in a prior message */
+        PK_sender = verified_keys[claimed_sender]
+    else:
+        /* First message from this sender: verify their certificate */
+        cert_digest = HASH(cert.identity || cert.PK)
+        if not VERIFY(cert_digest, cert.CA_sig, PK_CA):
+            REJECT("Certificate forged or corrupted")
+            return
+        if cert.identity != claimed_sender:
+            REJECT("Identity mismatch")
+            return
+        PK_sender = cert.PK
+        verified_keys[claimed_sender] = PK_sender
+
+    /* Verify message integrity and authenticity (always required) */
+    h = HASH(M)
+    if not VERIFY(h, sig, PK_sender):
+        REJECT("Signature verification failed")
+        return
+
+    ACCEPT(M, claimed_sender)
+```
+
+This caching eliminates repeated certificate verification for subsequent messages from the same sender, saving one hash and one asymmetric verify per message. The per-message cost is reduced to just one hash of M and one signature verify — the minimum necessary to guarantee integrity and authenticity for each individual message.
+
+### Why This Scheme Is Correct
+
+**Against message modification:** A malicious user who modifies M in transit cannot forge a valid signature because they do not possess the sender's private key. The hash of the modified message will not match the signed hash, so VERIFY will fail.
+
+**Against message injection:** A malicious user who injects a new message cannot forge a valid signature from another user's private key. Even if they include a fabricated certificate, it will fail CA verification because the attacker cannot forge the CA's signature on an arbitrary (identity, public key) pair.
+
+**Against identity spoofing:** A malicious user cannot claim to be someone else because they cannot produce a certificate for that identity (cannot forge CA signature) and cannot sign with that person's private key.
